title: Making seed paper
subtitle: Embedding viable seeds in a handmade sheet that can be planted
excerpt: Seed paper is handmade paper with small seeds embedded in the pulp during forming. When planted in soil and kept moist, the sheet biodegrades and the seeds germinate. The key variables are seed selection (small seeds with low dormancy requirements), pulp type (biodegradable, no synthetic sizing), and drying temperature (never exceed 40°C or the seeds die).
sourceNotes: Dard Hunter, Papermaking: The History and Technique of an Ancient Craft (1943, public domain via Internet Archive), on the embedding of materials in handmade paper. The application to viable seeds is an extension of the decorative-inclusion tradition documented in Asian papermaking.

glossaryTerms.definition: The capacity of a seed to germinate under appropriate conditions. Viability is reduced by heat above 40°C, by excessive drying, and by prolonged storage after the paper is made.
The suspension of beaten plant fibre in water before forming. For seed paper, the slurry should be cooled to room temperature before seeds are added to protect viability.

A surface treatment such as dilute starch or no sizing at all that allows the sheet to break down in soil without leaving synthetic residues. Gelatine sizing is biodegradable; acrylic and PVA sizings are not.

content.text: Seed paper works best with small, light seeds that require no pre-treatment before germination: wildflower mixes, basil, chives, thyme, and viola are reliable choices. Avoid seeds that require cold stratification or soaking. The seeds are added to the
in the vat just before pulling, not during blending. Blending kills most seeds.

attrs.heading: Temperature rule
attrs.body: Seed viability is destroyed above 40°C. Do not use warm water in the vat and do not dry the sheets in an oven or on a radiator. Air-dry only, at room temperature.

items.name: Cotton linter
items.notes: Produces a clean, biodegradable base. Do not use pulps with synthetic additives.

items.name: Small seeds
items.notes: Wildflower mix, basil, thyme, or viola. 1 teaspoon per sheet is sufficient.

items.name: Papermaking vat with cold water
items.notes: The water must be at room temperature to protect seed viability.

content.text: Beat the cotton linter in cold water and fill the vat to the usual working level. Check the
condition: the slurry must be cold before seeds go in. Add the seeds and stir gently to distribute evenly through the vat. Pull sheets immediately; once seeds are in the vat the clock is running.

Pull sheets in the normal way. Seeds settle slightly faster than fibres, so stir the vat before each pull to keep them in suspension. Couch and press as normal. Do not apply gelatine or synthetic sizing: the finished sheet must be fully

Air-dry the pressed sheets at room temperature. Do not use heat. Once dry, store in a cool dry place. Seed paper keeps for one to two years if stored away from moisture and heat. Include planting instructions with the finished sheet: plant in damp compost, cover with 3 mm of soil, keep moist, and expect germination in 7 to 14 days for most wildflower seeds.

items.symptom: Seeds do not germinate when the sheet is planted
items.cause: Seeds were added to warm pulp or dried with heat.
items.fix: Use cold water throughout and air-dry only. Test viability before making a large batch by soaking a few seeds on damp kitchen roll for 48 hours.

items.symptom: Seeds sink and concentrate in the bottom half of the sheet
items.cause: The seeds are too heavy for the pulp concentration.
items.fix: Stir the vat immediately before each pull. Use lighter seeds (thyme, poppy) rather than heavier ones (sunflower, marigold).
